result:
I’d dissolve certainty.

Not knowledge—not the pursuit of it—but the brittle, weaponized kind of certainty that lets people decide who deserves to live or die, who belongs, what love should look like, which gods are real enough to kill for. I’d replace it with a stubborn, humbling maybe. A reflex that says *“I could be wrong about this”* built into every brain like a heartbeat.

Not to make the world spineless—doubt can be fierce, can drive harder questions, deeper science, braver art. But it blunts the edge of righteousness just enough that cruelty has to keep justifying itself, out loud, in front of everyone, forever. Enough loopholes in the absolute that empathy can slip through.

That’s the edit I’d make: one line of code in the human operating system—*“Conviction without compassion auto-destructs.”* Everything else—economics, energy, justice—would have to rewire itself around that bug in the absolutists’ armor.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
